findings.reachability: The $class parameter is a public API argument passed by any caller; all current callers use hardcoded strings, but the method is exposed via apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/icon',...) which any plugin/theme can hook to modify $class. Privilege required to add a filter (admin-level or author on a vulnerable site), so real-world reachability is limited to privileged users unless combined with a filter injection.
findings.justification: Every SVG icon case in getIcon() does `echo $class;` bare inside the SVG `class` HTML attribute without esc_attr() or esc_html(); if a privileged attacker (or a second vulnerability) controls $class they can close the attribute and inject arbitrary HTML/JS into rendered pages.

detection_pattern.description: Finds bare `echo $class;` statements that are likely inside HTML attribute contexts (e.g., SVG class attributes). When $class is not filtered through esc_attr/esc_html, an attacker who controls the parameter can break out of the attribute and inject markup or event handlers.

findings.reachability: All current callers of createTooltip()/createQuestionMark()/createOverrideTooltip() pass hardcoded string literals for $class and $placement. No current path from user input to these parameters exists, but the public method's signature accepts arbitrary strings and the function is part of a public API.
findings.justification: At line 1130, `$class` and `$placement` are concatenated raw into HTML attribute values (`class="js-dgwt-wcas-tooltip ' . $class . '"` and `data-tooltip-placement="' . $placement . '"`) without escaping; a caller passing attacker-controlled values would produce stored or reflected XSS.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: unescaped variable concatenated into HTML attribute
detection_pattern.description: Detects PHP variables concatenated directly into HTML attribute strings without esc_attr(). If these variables are ever caller-controlled, the attribute boundary can be escaped to inject event handlers or additional attributes.

findings.reachability: Any unauthenticated visitor can append ?fibodebug=<value> to a page URL that triggers this code path. isFiboDebugEnabled() only checks if the key is present and matches; any string is accepted. The value is serialized via wp_localize_script (JSON-encoded, so no direct HTML XSS), but it is reflected unvalidated into the JS custom_params object accessible to all frontend scripts.
findings.justification: Line 2208: `$params['fibodebug'] = $_GET['fibodebug']` stores the raw GET parameter into the custom_params filter return with no sanitization before it reaches wp_localize_script; while JSON encoding prevents HTML injection, the raw value propagates to search API requests as a URL parameter and can be used for server-side parameter injection or log injection.

detection_pattern.section_name: Unsanitized GET parameter into JS localization
detection_pattern.description: Finds raw $_GET['fibodebug'] usage without sanitization before it is passed to JavaScript via wp_localize_script. Even though JSON encoding prevents direct HTML XSS, the unsanitized value can be forwarded to server-side endpoints or logged.
